1:
The chapter opens with a word of judgment, and the witnesses first fix the identity of the messenger. The grammatical-historical commentators anchor him in a prophetic family. Ellicott takes Jehu the son of Hanani to be the son of Hanani the seer who had rebuked Asa of Judah (2 Chron. 16:7), and reckons Jehu still young, since he reappears long afterward rebuking Jehoshaphat and writing the annals of his reign (2 Chron. 19:2; 20:34). Gill, the lone Reformed voice, says the same: the son of a prophet then living, a young man whom we meet again years later as a reprover of kings and a writer of history.

The Puritan Matthew Henry reads the verse for its doctrine of warning. God sent Baasha notice of his ruin beforehand, Henry observes, both that he might repent and escape it — for God threatens that he may not strike, having no pleasure in the death of sinners — and that, if he would not, the destruction when it came might plainly appear as the act of God's justice and the punishment of sin. He notes the fitness of the envoy: the father had been sent to Asa, but the son, younger and more active, was dispatched on the longer and more dangerous errand to Baasha king of Israel. Juniores ad labores — toil and adventure are for the young. Prophecy thus happily entailed from father to son was worthy of the more honour, and Jehu's long usefulness, more than forty years in the work, confirms it.

The Wesleyan Adam Clarke is content to register how little is known: of this prophet we learn nothing but from this one circumstance, yet he too draws in the father, Hanani, who suffered imprisonment for the faithful discharge of his ministry to Asa (2 Chron. 16:7–10). The voices do not divide. Together they set a house of prophets over against a house of kings, and Henry catches the message's very shape, so like Ahijah's word to Jeroboam, which the following verses will unfold: a reminder of grace received, a charge of sin, and a sentence of doom. That God should stoop to warn a usurper and idolater at all is, for these expositors, the first surprising mercy of a chapter otherwise dark with blood.
2:
The indictment proper begins, and the witnesses hear in it the same two-part logic that had condemned Jeroboam: a reminder of unmerited elevation, then a charge of ingratitude and idolatry. The grammatical-historical commentators mark the parallel. Ellicott observes that the prophecy closely resembles Ahijah's word against Jeroboam, and that it plainly shows Baasha had a probation which he neglected; his guilt, hinted at in v.7, was enhanced by persevering in the very sins for which, by his own hand, so terrible a vengeance had been executed. Keil and Delitzsch simply lay the oracle out in full, letting its measured clauses — I exalted thee out of the dust, and made thee prince over my people Israel; and thou hast walked in the way of Jeroboam — carry their own weight.

The Reformed Gill fastens on the phrase out of the dust: God raised Baasha from a very low estate and a mean family to the throne of glory. Matthew Henry, whose fuller comment stands over the whole passage, draws the doctrine of providence out of it — Baasha seemed to have climbed by his own treachery and cruelty, yet there was a hand of Providence in it, echoing Hannah's confession that the Lord raiseth up the poor out of the dust (1 Sam. 2:8). Yet God's owning the advancement as his own act by no means patronizes the ambition and bloodshed that were the man's own; it is God who puts power into bad men's hands, making it serve his good purposes despite the evil use they make of it. And Israel, wretchedly corrupted, is still called my people, because the covenant stood and many godly remained among them — it was long after that they were named Loammi, not a people (Hos. 1:9).

The Wesleyan Clarke guards the same point against misunderstanding. Made thee prince over my people means only that in the course of his providence God suffered Baasha to become king; it is impossible that God should make a rebel, a traitor, and a murderer king over his people, and Scripture everywhere represents God as doing what, in his providence, he merely permits to be done. The voices are wholly agreed: the throne was a gift, the sin was Baasha's own, and the sentence about to fall is therefore just.
3:
The sentence itself is pronounced, and though only the Reformed Gill comments directly, the witnesses' shared reading of the surrounding oracle lets the verse speak fully. Gill glosses the threat plainly: I will take away the posterity of Baasha, and the posterity of his house means that by death there shall be none of his family remaining in any branch of it. The doom is total — not a chastening that prunes a dynasty, but an uprooting that leaves nothing to grow again.

What gives the verse its weight is the exactness of the retribution the wider passage insists upon. God says he will make Baasha's house like the house of Jeroboam the son of Nebat, and every witness in this chapter has felt the terrible symmetry of that comparison. Henry, over the passage, states the principle that governs it: those who resemble others in their sins may expect to resemble them in their plagues, especially those who show zeal against sins in others which they allow in themselves. Baasha had been the instrument that destroyed Jeroboam's line (v.7); now the measure he meted is measured back to him, so that the punishment answers the sin as face answers to face in a glass. The same law of just recompense would later fall on the house of Jehu for the blood of the house of Ahab (Hos. 1:4).

The word Behold arrests the hearer: the God who had lifted Baasha from the dust in v.2 now, in the same breath, announces the ruin of all that had sprung from him. The threat is dynastic because the sin was dynastic; Baasha had not merely sinned but had made Israel to sin, and a corruption sown in a whole kingdom is answered by a judgment that spares no heir. The reprieve granted to Baasha himself, who will die in peace in v.6, only sharpens the point: the stroke falls not on the man but on his seed, a pattern that, the witnesses agree, quietly testifies to a reckoning beyond the grave, where impenitent sinners suffer in their own persons and do not escape.
4:
The doom is now particularized into its most shameful detail, and again the Reformed Gill supplies the single direct note, though the whole company of witnesses has felt its horror. Him that dieth of Baasha in the city shall the dogs eat, and him that dieth of his in the fields shall the fowls of the air eat: Gill observes that this is exactly the same threat which was pronounced against, and executed upon, the family of Jeroboam. The curse is denial of burial — the body left to scavengers of street and sky, an abomination to a people for whom decent interment was a mark of covenant honour.

Henry, reflecting over the passage, presses the strange contrast the verse creates with the fate of Baasha himself. The father will be buried with honour in his own royal city (v.6), yet the threatening specifies for his house the very thing he escaped: that the dogs and the fowls should devour them. In this Henry hears a deliberate intimation. Baasha, the greatest delinquent, lives and dies under no visible stroke of vengeance; his children are punished after his death, and their bodies abused after theirs. If judgment can be deferred so long and then fall so precisely, there must be a future state in which impenitent sinners suffer in their own persons and do not, as they often seem to here, slip away untouched. God, he says with Job, layeth up a man's iniquity for his children (Job 21:19).

The verse thus does more than name a grisly end; it fixes the pattern of the chapter. The dishonoured corpse is the visible sign of a covenant broken, the outward token that these kings had made themselves, in the prophet's later word, mere vanities. And because the threat is one already fulfilled upon Jeroboam and about to be fulfilled upon Baasha's son through Zimri's sword (vv.11–12), the witnesses read it as proof that not one syllable of the word of the Lord falls to the ground. What the prophet speaks, history performs.
5:
The formula of the annalist now closes Baasha's record, and the Reformed Gill gives the one direct comment, observing that the rest of his acts, and his might, were written in the book of the chronicles of the kings of Israel, just as those of Jeroboam and Nadab had been. The grammatical-historical instinct that the other witnesses bring to such notices is present here in Gill's brief cross-reference: this is the standing editorial refrain by which the compiler of Kings points beyond his own selective narrative to fuller state records now lost.

The phrase his might repays a moment's weight. Baasha was, by every account in the chapter, an active, politic, and daring man — Henry calls him one likely enough to have raised and established his family. The historian grants him his strength; kings of energy and success fill the annals of the world. Yet the very next verses will show that might and memorial are not the same as acceptance with God. Many a bad man has been a stout man, and his name found in the chronicles of nations; but, as the witnesses repeatedly draw out over this chapter, such a name is not written in the book of life. The chronicle of the kings preserves the deeds; it does not absolve them.

There is, too, a quiet judgment in the very brevity of the notice. A reign that had begun in the blood of Jeroboam's house and provoked God with a whole kingdom's idolatry is dismissed in a single reference to a book. The prophet's oracle in vv.2–4 had already told the reader what heaven made of this reign; the chronicler's formula tells what earth would remember of it — the acts and the might, but not one word of penitence. Between the divine verdict and the human record the verse leaves a telling silence, and the reader is meant to feel it as Baasha sleeps with his fathers in the next line. The compiler’s very economy is a kind of sermon: a life may fill the annals of a nation and yet leave behind nothing that heaven will own, its recorded deeds neither cancelling its sins nor pleading for its memory.
6:
Baasha's end is recorded with the settled phrase of Hebrew narrative, and the Reformed Gill notes the plain sense: So Baasha slept with his fathers — that is, he died, and died not a violent but a natural death, and was buried in Tirzah, the royal city. After a reign begun in treason and steeped in idolatry, the king himself goes down to the grave in peace and with honour.

It is this very peacefulness that the Puritan Matthew Henry seizes as the theological heart of the passage. The threat of v.4 had consigned Baasha's house to the dogs and the fowls; yet Baasha, the chief offender, is so far from being a prey to either that he dies quietly in his bed and is laid with his fathers. He lives not to see or feel the punishment denounced upon his line. Henry finds the lesson unavoidable: since the greatest delinquent escapes all visible stroke of divine vengeance in this world, there must certainly be a future state in which impenitent sinners suffer in their own persons and do not slip away, as here they often seem to do. God laid up Baasha's iniquity for his children, and reserved the man's own account for a day beyond the grave, when he who has power to kill has power also to cast into hell.

The verse also serves the chapter's larger irony. Baasha had risen out of the dust by God's providence and now returns to it undisturbed, while his son Elah, mentioned here as reigning in his stead, will within two years be cut down drunk at a servant's table (v.9–10). The father's honourable burial and the son's dishonoured corpse stand side by side, and the witnesses read the pairing as a sober warning: a comfortable death is no proof of a comfortable eternity, and the peace of the wicked in this life is the reprieve, not the pardon, of a patient God. Tirzah gives the king an honourable grave; the prophet had promised his house a shameful one; and between the two the witnesses read the whole distance between what men see of a sinner’s end and what God has appointed for it.
7:
The historian returns a second time to Jehu's oracle, and the witnesses read the repetition as deliberate, differing gently over why it is added. The Reformed Gill, following Abarbanel, suggests that the earlier statement of the prophecy had threatened Baasha chiefly because he made Israel to sin, whereas this notice grounds the doom in his own evil works — here it is because of the work of his hands in provoking the Lord to anger, and because he killed him, that is, because he had destroyed the house of Jeroboam.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott hears the second reference as a note of the historian himself, added chiefly for the sake of the last clause, which shows that Baasha's act — though it fulfilled a prophecy — was not thereby justified. This is the sharp moral point of the verse, and it draws out the very tension Henry had raised over v.2: Baasha, in exterminating Jeroboam's line, had unwittingly executed the justice of God, yet he did it as the servant of his own lust and ambition, not from any holy indignation against Jeroboam's sin. Had he acted with an eye to God's will and glory, he would have been accepted as a minister of divine justice; acting from malice, he was but the tool of that justice and is justly punished for the temper in which he wrought. Those employed to denounce or execute God's judgments, Henry warns, must do it from a good principle and in a holy manner, lest the very work turn into sin against them.

The Wesleyan Clarke turns aside to a textual and syntactic puzzle. The clause and because he killed him he notes the Vulgate refers to Jehu the prophet, as though Baasha had put the seer to death; others read it of Jeroboam or of Nadab Jeroboam's son, the last being the view of Jarchi and some good critics. Clarke observes that the order here is confused and thinks v.7 should probably stand between v.4 and v.5. The exegetical uncertainty is real but narrow; on the substance the witnesses agree that the verse exists to forestall a fatal excuse — that a man may sin freely so long as his crime happens to serve God's hidden purpose.
8:
The narrative crosses to the next reign, and the Reformed Gill supplies the chronology with characteristic care: in the twenty-sixth year of Asa king of Judah, Elah the son of Baasha began to reign over Israel in Tirzah, and reigned two years — not two complete years, Gill notes, for he died in the twenty-seventh of Asa. The detail is exact, and Gill draws the ominous parallel that the whole chapter is built upon: Elah reigned just the length of time that Nadab the son of Jeroboam had reigned before Baasha cut him off.

That parallel is the quiet engine of the passage. The prophet had sworn to make Baasha's house like the house of Jeroboam (v.3), and the correspondence begins to unfold with almost mechanical precision. As Jeroboam's dynasty passed to a son who held the throne barely two years before falling to a captain's conspiracy, so Baasha's passes to Elah, who will hold it exactly as long and fall in exactly the same way. The grammatical-historical concern for the synchronism between the reigns of Israel and Judah — the dating by the twenty-sixth year of Asa — is here served by Gill's insistence that the two years are reckoned inclusively, so that the numbers of the following verses cohere.

The verse gives no character sketch of Elah; what little we learn of him comes from the manner of his death in v.9, where he is found drinking himself drunk while his army besieges Gibbethon. Yet the bare notice of his accession already casts its shadow. A dynasty founded in blood and idolatry has produced an heir who inherits both the throne and the sentence hanging over it; his two short years are a reprieve running out. The witnesses read the terse chronological formula not as a neutral date but as the ticking of the clock the prophet had set going — the house of Baasha now standing precisely where the house of Jeroboam had stood on the eve of its extinction. Gill’s small correction, that the two years were not complete, is thus no idle scruple: it fixes how brief the reprieve was, and how exactly the son’s span would answer to Nadab’s before the sword fell.
9:
The conspiracy breaks, and the witnesses converge on the squalid circumstances of Elah's fall. The grammatical-historical commentators supply the setting. Keil and Delitzsch identify Zimri as commander of half the king's war-chariots, who not only slew Elah when he was intoxicated at a drinking bout in the house of Arza the steward of the palace, but afterward exterminated the whole house of Baasha; the steward, they judge, was probably party to the plot and had arranged the carousal in his own house to bring it off. Ellicott sharpens the moral contrast with a note of half-contemptuous condemnation: there is an emphasis in the description of Elah's public debauchery — drunk in the house of a mere household officer while war raged at Gibbethon — set against Zimri, marked emphatically as his servant, the high officer left in charge of the palace and the king's person while the army was in the field. Hence, Ellicott adds, Zimri's name passed into a proverb for unusual treachery (2 Kings 9:31).

The Reformed Gill and the Wesleyan Clarke fill in the military detail: there were two captains of the chariots, and Zimri commanded one half — the cavalry, Clarke supposes — so that the traitor was no obscure figure but a trusted commander close to the throne.

Yet the verse's abiding force, for these witnesses, lies in its picture of a king surprised in his sin. Matthew Henry, over the passage, turns Elah into a warning to drunkards, and especially to those who deliberately drink themselves drunk: they know not but death may seize them in that very condition. Death comes easily upon men when they are drunk, unable to help themselves and easily overcome by an enemy; and it comes terribly, finding them in the act of sin and unfit for any act of devotion, so that the day comes upon them unawares, as a snare (Luke 21:34). Elah should have been at the head of his army before Gibbethon; he loved his ease and his cup better than his honour, and the cup was his death. The witnesses do not divide: a king surprised drunk in a subject’s house, while his soldiers fought his war, is for them the very portrait of a throne that has forfeited both dignity and defence.
10:
The blow falls, and the witnesses record it with the same terse precision as the text. The Reformed Gill notes simply that Zimri went in and smote Elah and killed him when in his drunken fit, and reigned in his stead, dating the deed to the twenty-seventh year of Asa. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch set the murder within the fuller sweep of what followed: Zimri, as soon as he sat on the throne, slew all the house of Baasha and left him not one that pisseth against a wall — a phrase, they explain by cross-reference (1 Kings 14:10), that restricts the slaughter to the males, kinsfolk and friends alike, so that no avenger of blood might remain.

The killing of a drunken king by a trusted captain completes the correspondence the chapter has been tracing. Just as Baasha had struck down Nadab and destroyed the whole house of Jeroboam, so now Zimri strikes down Elah and destroys the whole house of Baasha. The instrument of judgment has himself become the object of the next judgment's instrument; the very violence by which Baasha's line had risen is the violence by which it falls. The witnesses see in the exact repetition not the working of blind fortune but the unfolding of the prophetic word of v.3, which v.12 will name explicitly.

There is a further note the passage cannot suppress: Zimri gains a throne he will hold for seven days. The reader who watches him reign in Elah's stead already knows, from the shape of the chapter, that a man who seizes a crown by such treachery over such a corpse has secured nothing durable. The suddenness with which Zimri rises — a single stroke in a private house — foreshadows the suddenness with which he will fall when the army before Gibbethon hears what he has done. The verse thus stands as both an execution of God's sentence on Baasha and the opening of Zimri's own brief and doomed usurpation. The two witnesses, brief as they are, thus hold together the chapter’s double lesson: the certainty of the prophetic word that dooms Baasha’s house, and the swiftness with which the executioner of that word makes himself the next man marked for judgment.
11:
The extent of Zimri's slaughter is spelled out, and the witnesses dwell on its thoroughness. The Reformed Gill marks the haste of it — as soon as he sat on his throne, perhaps the very first day — and the Wesleyan Clarke the completeness: Zimri endeavoured to exterminate the whole race and to blot out its very memory. Clarke adds a rabbinic touch, that when such a sentence of extirpation was determined the Jews said the executioners destroyed not only the man's own house but the five neighbouring houses, that his memory might perish from the earth.

The verse's own phrasing carries the horror the commentators feel. Zimri left Baasha neither his kinsfolks nor his friends, a clause the witnesses take, with Keil and Delitzsch on v.10, to describe the killing of every male who might one day avenge the blood. Here treachery consolidates itself by butchery: Zimri holds by cruelty what he had gained by treason, and his purge reaches further than Baasha's own had done against the house of Jeroboam, sparing not even the friends of the fallen king.

Matthew Henry, reflecting over the whole scene, presses the irony that will overtake the murderer. Divine justice avenged Elah's blood so swiftly and remarkably that Zimri's name became a byword long after — Had Zimri peace, who slew his master? (2 Kings 9:31). The very completeness of his revenge upon Baasha's house could not purchase him a week's security. And beneath the political calculation the witnesses see the hand of God: this bloody extermination, however cruelly meant by Zimri, is the exact fulfilment of the doom pronounced by Jehu (v.3–4), so that the usurper who imagined he was founding a dynasty was in fact discharging a prophecy against the house he destroyed — and sealing, by the manner of it, the ruin of his own. The witnesses hold the two truths side by side without strain: the slaughter is Zimri’s guilt, freely chosen and cruelly extended even to friends, and it is God’s justice, exactly measured to the house that had itself risen by the blood of Jeroboam. The murderer heaps up wrath in the very act by which he fulfils a righteous sentence.
12:
The historian pauses to interpret the carnage, and though the Reformed Gill alone comments directly, his note catches the theological key the whole chapter turns upon. Thus did Zimri destroy all the house of Baasha, according to the word of the Lord which he spake against Baasha by Jehu the prophet: Gill observes that the destruction reached not only Baasha's posterity but all in any way related to him, and seems even to have extended to those bound to him in friendship. The slaughter that v.11 described as Zimri's cruelty is here re-described as God's word performed.

This is the decisive interpretive move of the passage, and it is one the grammatical-historical witnesses make habitually: the same event is at once a human crime and a divine fulfilment. Keil and Delitzsch, treating the verse, refer the phrase according to the word of the Lord back to the oracle of v.1, and read the ground of the doom in the following clause — it was for all the sins of Baasha, and because they had provoked God with their vanities, that is, with the idols by which the golden calves are meant. The judgment is not arbitrary; it answers a named sin.

What the verse forbids is any thought that the prophecy excused the executioner. Zimri acted from ambition and shed innocent blood, and for that he will perish within days; yet in the mysterious economy of providence his very treason discharged a sentence that heaven had justly passed. The witnesses hold both truths without flinching, as Ellicott had insisted over v.7 that a foretold act is not thereby justified. Here the accent falls on the other side of that same truth: God's word does not fail. The house lifted out of the dust in v.2 is now wholly returned to it, precisely as the prophet had sworn, and the reader is left to marvel that the God who governs the crimes of usurpers to fulfil his righteous word governs them still. The verse, brief as it is, thus states the chapter’s controlling conviction: history is not a chaos of ambitious men cutting one another down, but the theatre in which the word spoken by a prophet is, to the last kinsman and friend, performed.
13:
The historian names the sins for which the house fell, and the witnesses gather on the twin culprits and the word that damns them. The Reformed Gill draws the first inference from the phrase and the sins of Elah his son: it appears the son trod in his father's steps, and was therefore cut off with him — the doom was not visited on an innocent heir but on a partner in the guilt. The Wesleyan Clarke states the passage's plain purpose: here we see why God permitted such judgments to fall on this family; Baasha was a grievous offender, and Elah with him, and they made the people to sin and provoked God to anger with their idolatries.

The grammatical-historical commentators fasten on the loaded word vanities. Keil and Delitzsch gloss the Hebrew through their nothingnesses, that is, their idols, by which the golden calves are meant. Ellicott expands it into the Old Testament's whole theology of idolatry: vanities is the standing scriptural name for idols (Deut. 32:21; 1 Sam. 12:21; Ps. 31:6; Isa. 41:29), and it covers not only the calves of Dan and Bethel but the worse abominations that grew up under cover of them. In the Old Testament, Ellicott observes, the contempt for idolatry as a gross folly — faith wasted upon unrealities — is at least as strong as the condemnation of it as an outrage against God's law; witness the utter scorn poured on the idol-maker in Isa. 44:9–20 and Ps. 115:4–8.

The verse thus does more than assign blame; it defines the essence of these kings' offence. To forsake the living God for images is not only wicked but pitiable, a bartering of the Rock for a nothing. The witnesses do not divide. Together they read the fall of Baasha and Elah as the just wages of a sin that is at once the deepest insult to God and the emptiest folly in man — miserable, as Henry says elsewhere in the chapter, are those whose gods are vanities, for such gods can neither profit nor help.
14:
Elah's brief reign is closed with the annalist's refrain, and the Reformed Gill gives the sole direct note, pointing the reader — as the formula itself does — to the fuller record in the book of the chronicles of the kings of Israel, where the rest of Elah's acts were written. The grammatical-historical sensitivity to this recurring editorial device, already seen at Baasha's death in v.5, applies here too: the compiler of Kings is selective by design, recording what serves his prophetic and theological purpose and referring the merely political detail elsewhere.

What strikes the witnesses is how little there is to close. Baasha's obituary in v.5 could at least speak of his might; Elah's says only that his acts are written, with no word of strength or achievement. The son who inherited the throne and the sentence together leaves behind a reign summed up by the manner of its end — a king drunk at a servant's table while his soldiers fought, struck down before he had done anything the historian cares to name. The very thinness of the notice is a kind of verdict.

The formula also quietly seals the fulfilment the chapter has been tracing. With Elah gone and his house destroyed, the parallel the prophet had sworn between the line of Baasha and the line of Jeroboam (v.3) is complete: two dynasties, each of a founder and a short-lived son, each ended by a captain's conspiracy, each dismissed at last with the same chronicler's words. The book of the chronicles keeps the outward acts; the book of Kings keeps the meaning of them. And the meaning, the witnesses agree, is that a reign spent provoking God with vanities earns no memorial worth the name — only a reference to a record, and a place in the long catalogue of Israel's kings who did evil and passed away. The witnesses read the very sparseness of the notice as the fitting close to a house that had provoked God with vanities: it rose in blood, it reigned in idolatry, and it vanishes into a cross-reference, leaving the reader to weigh how little a crown is worth that is not held in the fear of God.
15:
Zimri's seven days open, and the witnesses read the verse as the hinge on which the chapter's chaos turns. The Reformed Gill states the bare span — Zimri reigned seven days in Tirzah, until Omri came and took the palace and destroyed him — while the army lay encamped against Gibbethon, the Philistine city. The Wesleyan Clarke supplies the strategic frame: Israel was then besieging Gibbethon, and the host, hearing of Zimri's treason, made their general Omri king, who at once raised the siege to march on the royal city of Tirzah.

The Puritan Matthew Henry, whose long reflection governs the whole Omri narrative, opens it here with Solomon's maxim: for the transgression of a land many are the princes thereof (Prov. 28:2), so it was in Israel now, while by a man of understanding Judah under Asa enjoyed prolonged stability. When men forsake God, Henry observes, they are out of the way of rest and settlement; here Zimri, Tibni, and Omri strive for the crown, proud aspiring men ruining one another and drawing others into the ruin. He marks the recurring irony that the Philistines are sure to gain whenever the Israelites quarrel: the siege of Gibbethon, first fatal to Nadab (1 Kings 15:27), is now abandoned a second time as the army turns from the enemy to avenge a murdered king upon a traitor.

Beneath the political turmoil the witnesses hear the note of retribution. Zimri had reigned seven days, Henry observes, so long before Omri was proclaimed king and Zimri himself proclaimed traitor; yet even in that week he showed his inclination to the way of Jeroboam and made himself obnoxious to God's justice (v.19). The army that would not tamely submit to a regicide becomes, unwittingly, the rod of a further judgment. The verse thus gathers the chapter's forces for its next convulsion: a king of seven days penned in his capital, a general lifted up by the sword, and a kingdom that, having forsaken its God, can find no rest but by the ruin of one aspirant after another.
16:
The army acclaims a new king, and the witnesses read the scene as the naked triumph of the sword in Israel's politics. The Reformed Gill notes only that the tidings of Zimri's conspiracy and regicide reached the camp and displeased the host; but the grammatical-historical commentators draw out the constitutional meaning. Ellicott observes that this sudden exaltation of Omri, as a matter of course, shows how entirely the kingdom of Israel had become the prize of the sword. He notes a striking coincidence — by comparison with 1 Kings 15:27, the dynasty of Baasha itself had been founded in the camp before this very city of Gibbethon, so that history repeats its own violence on the same ground. Zimri's conspiracy, Ellicott adds, appears to have been hastily planned with no provision of adequate support, for Tirzah falls at once.

Keil and Delitzsch fill in the mechanism: as soon as the people of war heard of Zimri's plot, usurpation, and murders, they proclaimed Omri, the captain of the host, king in the camp, and he straightway broke off the siege of Gibbethon to march with all Israel — that is, all the army — against Tirzah. The people of Israel in these verses, they note, are essentially the fighting people; the succession is decided by the military.

The witnesses do not divide, and their agreement carries a somber judgment. A throne that changes hands by assassination and is filled by acclamation of an army in the field is a throne stripped of every sanction but force. Henry's larger reflection frames it: when tyrants succeed one another, and massacres, conspiracies, and civil wars follow, we may be sure the Lord has a controversy with a people for their sins, and they are loudly called to repent. The verse records not merely a coup but the symptom of a kingdom under divine displeasure, where the crown has become the spoil of whoever commands the most swords — a condition that will not end until Omri has crushed both Zimri and Tibni. The witnesses read the camp’s hasty acclamation, then, as no true settlement but the mere transfer of a contested crown from one soldier’s hand to another, the outward sign of an inward judgment that no change of dynasty could lift.
17:
Omri moves against the usurper, and the Reformed Gill gives the campaign in a sentence: Omri and the army under him broke up the siege of Gibbethon and marched to Tirzah — some thirty-six miles distant, on the reckoning Gill cites from Bunting — and besieged the royal city in which Zimri had shut himself. The verse is spare, but its speed is the point: the whole weight of the national army, lately turned against the Philistines, wheels about in a single stride to fall upon a king of seven days.

The grammatical-historical observation that Ellicott had made over v.16 is borne out here — Zimri's conspiracy had made no provision of adequate support, so that Tirzah is invested at once and cannot hold. There is no battle in the open field, no rallying of a faction to the usurper; the man who had seized the crown in a private house by a single murder now finds the crown's whole military strength arrayed against him under a rival general. The march from Gibbethon to Tirzah is the measure of how little seven days of stolen royalty could buy.

The witnesses read the verse as the swift outworking of the retribution already set in motion. Zimri had held by cruelty what he gained by treason (v.11); now the same violence that carried him to the throne closes upon him without delay. And beneath the tactics the larger providence is at work: the army that abandoned a legitimate war to pursue a domestic vengeance becomes, as Henry sees throughout this section, the instrument by which God's controversy with a rebellious kingdom is pressed home. The siege of Tirzah is the noose drawing tight, and v.18 will show the trapped usurper choosing the flames rather than surrender. Gill’s single sentence carries more than its length: the naming of the distance from Gibbethon to Tirzah measures the reach of an army that could abandon a foreign war in a day to punish a domestic crime, and the ease with which the royal city is besieged shows how little a throne seized by murder can command when the sword that made it turns against it.
18:
Cornered, Zimri destroys himself, and the witnesses read the desperate act for both its history and its meaning. The Reformed Gill sets the scene plainly: seeing that the city was taken — Omri and the army being within, Tirzah being ill-fortified and Zimri lacking force to hold it — the usurper went into the palace and burned the king's house over himself. The grammatical-historical Ellicott adds precision and depth. The phrase rendered palace, he notes, is the same found in 2 Kings 15:25, and means properly the high place or citadel of the building; some render it the harem, with which the Septuagint's curious word for a lurking-place might agree, though the term itself does not suggest it.

Ellicott presses the moral significance of the suicide. This desperate act, he observes, which has many parallels in Eastern history, seems to indicate that there was held to be something especially treasonable, and therefore unpardonable, in Zimri's assassination of Elah — a guilt so notorious (compare v.20 and 2 Kings 9:31) that the traitor could expect no mercy and preferred the fire to capture. Better to perish by his own hand in the flames than to fall, alive or dead, into the hands of an avenging army.

Matthew Henry, over the passage, draws the pathos and the warning. Unwilling that his rival should ever enjoy that sumptuous palace, Zimri burned it; and fearing to be ignominiously handled by the army, he burned himself in it. See, Henry says, what desperate courses men's wickedness sometimes drives them to, and how sin hurries them into their own ruin — the incendiary disposition that sets palaces and kingdoms ablaze though it perish in the flame. The witnesses do not divide: Zimri's self-immolation is at once the just end of a seven-day tyranny and a lurid emblem of the self-destroying nature of sin, which, when it has nothing left to seize, consumes the sinner himself. The witnesses read the burning palace as the fitting emblem of the whole seven-day reign: kindled in blood, it ends in fire, and the usurper who had made himself king by destroying another perishes at last by destroying himself and the very seat of the royalty he had grasped.
19:
The historian will not let even a seven-day reign pass without its verdict, and the witnesses read the epitaph as proof that idolatry, not merely treason, sealed Zimri's doom. The Reformed Gill notes that the sins which he sinned in doing evil in the sight of the Lord reach back into the former part of his life as well as forward, so that his death is punishment for a whole course, not only for the crime of his last week. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch tie the clause tightly to the preceding line: v.19 connects with the and he died of v.18, so that the sense is and so died for his sins, his self-inflicted death being the punishment of them.

The puzzle the witnesses feel is that so brief a usurper should be charged with walking in the way of Jeroboam, when he scarcely had time to walk anywhere. Ellicott offers two readings. The recurring phrase may indicate only the historian's sense of the curse lying on the whole kingdom through its idolatry, which Zimri, in his few days, did nothing to repudiate. Or — more pointedly — his conspiracy may have clothed itself under a pretence of righteous zeal for fulfilling Jehu's prophecy against Baasha (vv.3–4), and then thrown off the religious mask once the deed was done; for except in some such way he had no time to walk in Jeroboam's way at all.

Either way the verdict is the same, and the witnesses find in it a searching lesson. A man may seize a throne in the name of executing God's judgment and yet be condemned for the very idolatry he pretended to oppose. Zimri's crown lasted a week, but the charge against him is the charge that runs the length of the chapter — the calf-worship of Jeroboam, from which not one of these kings turned. His flames consumed a usurper who had made no more provision for his soul than for his throne, and the historian's terse for his sins fixes the true reason of his ruin beneath the political catastrophe.
20:
Zimri, too, receives the annalist's closing formula, and the witnesses note the pointed phrase that distinguishes it. The Reformed Gill observes that the record embraces what Zimri did both before and after his usurpation, during the seven days he was king, together with the manner of his conspiracy and his success in it. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch simply carry the formula into the next scene, where the kingdom divides between Tibni and Omri.

What arrests the commentators is the word treason set into the formula itself: the rest of the acts of Zimri, and his treason that he wrought. For the other kings the refrain speaks neutrally of acts and might; for Zimri the chronicler cannot name his record without naming his crime. The seven-day reign has no might to report and no deeds to catalogue but the one dark deed by which it began. His memorial is his treachery, and the historian's phrasing is itself a judgment — the same treachery that Henry, over v.11, saw pass into a proverb, Had Zimri peace, who slew his master? (2 Kings 9:31).

The verse also marks a threshold. With Zimri disposed of, the narrative turns from the swift collapse of one usurper to the longer struggle for the throne between Omri and Tibni. The reader who has watched three kings fall in quick succession — Elah murdered, Zimri burned, the house of Baasha extinguished — is being prepared for a contest that will last some years and cost much blood before the kingdom finds even the grim stability of Omri's dynasty. Zimri's brief and bloody parenthesis closes here, remembered by the very word that condemns him; and the witnesses read his epitaph as one more instance of the chapter's steady refrain, that the wages of forsaking God is a throne no one can keep. The witnesses read even this closing formula as part of the chapter’s argument: the historian will grant a king his record, but he will name it truly, and where there is nothing to name but treason he says so, letting the very shape of the obituary pronounce the sentence that Zimri’s flames had already carried out.
21:
The kingdom splits, and the witnesses turn to the causes and the significance of the four-year rift. The Reformed Gill notes plainly that the people of Israel were divided into two parts about the succession, while the Wesleyan Clarke confesses the reason is lost to us: why the division took place we cannot tell; the people appear to have been for Tibni, the army for Omri, and the latter prevailed. The line drawn is between popular and military allegiance, and, as Henry reflects over the passage, those who followed Tibni may have been the party unwilling to have a king chosen in the camp, lest he rule by the sword and a standing army, and who wished the succession settled instead by a convention of the states.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott supplies what history allows and marks what it withholds. Of Tibni we know nothing, he says; no doubt he too was a military chief, possibly Zimri's colleague under the supreme command of Omri, and the Septuagint even speaks of a brother, Joram, who fought and fell with him. Ellicott finds an ominous significance in the terse alternatives with which v.22 will close the struggle — so Tibni died, and Omri reigned — and, comparing the regnal dates of v.23 with v.15, reckons that the contest had lasted some four years. Keil and Delitzsch agree on the reckoning: Omri did not gain undisputed sovereignty at once, but only after about four years of civil war, the people of Israel here being probably the fighting men, so that the throne was again decided by the military.

The witnesses read the division as one more symptom of the disorder Henry had named from Solomon — a land whose transgression multiplies its princes. Two rival kings, a nation split in half, and years of internecine bloodshed are the price Israel pays for forsaking its God; and the terse verdict awaiting in the next verse, that one man simply died and the other reigned, exposes how little law and how much force now governed the choosing of a king in the northern kingdom. The rift is thus, for the witnesses, no mere accident of a disputed succession but the visible fracture of a people that had broken with its God, paying in years of civil bloodshed for a throne that could be settled by nothing gentler than war.
22:
The civil war is decided, and the witnesses weigh both the fact and the phrase that records it. The Reformed Gill supposes, reasonably, that a battle was fought in which Tibni's followers were worsted, for the people that followed Omri prevailed. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch use the verse to settle the chronology of the whole passage: Omri's twelve years (v.23) are reckoned from his rebellion against Zimri, so that comparing v.15 and 16 with 23 and 29 yields about four years of conflict with Tibni and only some seven or eight years of sole rule — he reigned six years in Tirzah, four during the struggle and two after Tibni's death.

Ellicott, over the previous verse, had already fastened on the grim economy of the historian's language, and it lands here: so Tibni died, and Omri reigned. Four years of bloodshed, a rival king, a nation torn in two, all compressed into six words that pass over the manner of Tibni's death in silence — whether in battle, as Gill guesses, or otherwise, the text does not say. The terseness is itself eloquent. In the northern kingdom a claimant to the throne is now simply an obstacle that dies; the survivor reigns not by right or by choice of the people but because his party prevailed in arms.

The witnesses read the verse as the close of the chapter's long season of anarchy. Since Baasha's death the crown had passed through Elah, Zimri, and a four-year interregnum of divided allegiance; now at last it rests, by conquest, on one head. Yet the settlement brings no relief to the deeper malady. Omri has won a throne, but, as the following verses will show, he will use it to do worse than all who were before him. The man of understanding by whom a state is prolonged (Henry's word from Prov. 28:2) is not found here; only a strong soldier who has outlasted his rivals, and whose triumph opens a dynasty darker than the ones it replaced. The witnesses read the six-word close as the epitaph of the whole convulsion: a rival simply dies, a survivor simply reigns, and the reader is left to feel how far Israel had fallen from any settled order when the crown passed, once more, not by covenant or counsel but by the outcome of a war between two soldiers.
23:
Omri's reign is formally dated, and the witnesses labour chiefly to reconcile its numbers. The Reformed Gill explains that the twelve years cannot be reckoned from the thirty-first of Asa — for Ahab succeeded in Asa's thirty-eighth year, which would leave Omri only seven or eight — but from the twenty-seventh of Asa, when Elah was slain and Omri first proclaimed, the division of the kingdom having lasted four years (Jarchi says five) before Tibni's death in the thirty-first, from which his sole reign is separately counted. The Wesleyan Clarke feels the same difficulty more sharply, noting that v.10 and v.15 place Zimri's usurpation in Asa's twenty-seventh year; he adopts Jarchi's solution from the Seder Olam — the divided kingdom began in Asa's twenty-seventh year and lasted some years, and Omri's undisputed sole reign began in the thirty-first, so that the twenty-seventh and thirty-first mark the beginning of the division and the beginning of the sole reign respectively.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott lifts the eye from the arithmetic to the epoch it opens. The accession of Omri after this long civil war, he observes, began a new age of more settled government and prosperity for about forty-eight years. Omri had to purchase peace with Syria by acknowledging its sovereignty and ceding cities (1 Kings 20:34); he allied himself with the royal house of Tyre, both for strength against Syria and for the revival of Solomon's old commercial prosperity, and he set about founding a new capital in a strong position. The phrase the might that he shewed marks him a warrior.

Yet Ellicott closes with the moral the witnesses share: probably, like Jeroboam and Baasha before him, Omri too had his opportunity of restoring the spiritual strength of his people by returning to the pure worship of God, and threw it away, doing worse than all who were before him. The careful chronology, then, is not pedantry but the frame for a tragedy — a capable founder of a lasting dynasty who used his settled power only to deepen the apostasy of Israel. So the harmonizing of the numbers, over which Gill and Clarke labour, serves the same end as Ellicott’s survey of the new epoch: to set precisely in time a reign that had every worldly advantage and squandered the one advantage that would have saved it.
24:
Omri founds his capital, and the witnesses give this verse their fullest antiquarian attention, for Samaria will shape the rest of the northern kingdom's story. The Reformed Gill records the price — two talents of silver, which he reckons, following Brerewood, at about seven hundred and fifty pounds — and the grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch the sum in their own currency, adding that Omri named the town Shomron after Shemer, the former owner of the hill, and made it his residence, where he and his successors reigned and were buried until Shalmaneser destroyed the kingdom (2 Kings 18:9–10).

The Wesleyan Clarke corrects the spelling to draw out the sense: the hill of Shomeron was bought from Shomer and named after him, and Omri, seeking to move the seat of government from Tirzah — scarred by civil broil and burnt in Zimri's fire — founded a new city there. Clarke then traces Samaria's whole after-history: adorned and fortified by the kings of Israel, holding Ahab's house of ivory (1 Kings 22:39) and the Syrian magazines (1 Kings 20:34), enduring the sieges of Ben-hadad (1 Kings 20:1) and the three-year siege of Shalmaneser (2 Kings 17:6), later rebuilt by Herod as Sebaste in honour of Augustus, and now reduced to a poor village.

Ellicott gathers the significance the others scatter. Omri only followed the usual practice of a new Eastern dynasty in founding a fresh capital, as Jeroboam had at Shechem; but the site of Samaria, he judges, must have been chosen by a soldier's eye. Its Hebrew name means a watch-tower, and may have a double derivation from its natural strength as well as its owner. Standing on an isolated hill with steep sides, in a basin of the Ephraim highlands near the edge of the maritime plain and commanding a view of the sea, its position was one of beauty and, in the warfare of that age, of singular strength, vindicated by the long sieges it withstood. The witnesses do not divide; together they see in Samaria the enduring monument of Omri's political sagacity — and, as the coming verses make plain, the future stronghold of Israel's Baal-worship.
25:
The historian delivers his verdict on Omri, and the witnesses probe the terrible superlative — he did worse than all that were before him. The Reformed Gill hears in the phrase an open, defiant impiety: Omri wrought evil openly and publicly, as if in defiance of God. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch note that whereas the former kings had only perpetuated Jeroboam's calf-worship, Omri went further and acted worse than all his predecessors on the throne.

The commentators labour to say wherein the worse consisted, for Omri, unlike Ahab, is charged with no new god. The Wesleyan Clarke offers the fullest analysis: Omri was an idolater in principle and in practice, led the people to idolatry by precept and example, and — the thing in which he outdid all before him — made statutes in favour of idolatry, obliging the people by law to commit it. Here Clarke points, as Ellicott and Henry do, to the prophet Micah's damning phrase, the statutes of Omri are kept (Micah 6:16), set in parallel with the works of the house of Ahab as the very symbol of hardened and hopeless apostasy. Ellicott adds that the superlative, used of Jeroboam himself in 1 Kings 14:9, may here indicate, beyond the old idolatry, some anticipation of the worse Baal-worship that Ahab would formally introduce.

Henry's larger reflection over the passage draws the moral the witnesses share. Though Omri was brought to the throne with much difficulty and providence had remarkably favoured his advancement, he proved more profane, or more superstitious, and a greater persecutor than the houses of Jeroboam or Baasha before him. Where Jeroboam had caused Israel to sin by temptation, example, and allurement, Omri did it by compulsion, establishing iniquity by a law. The witnesses do not divide: Omri's distinctive wickedness was to make apostasy a matter of statute, coercing a whole people into the sin that his predecessors had only seduced them into — a legal establishment of idolatry so grievous that a prophet would name it, generations later, as the measure of a nation ripe for desolation.
26:
The verdict on Omri is grounded in the familiar charge, and the witnesses read the recurring formula as the historian's settled diagnosis of the northern kingdom's disease. The Reformed Gill glosses it simply: Omri walked in the way of Jeroboam the son of Nebat and in his sin, that is, in worshipping the golden calves. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch note that the verse repeats, almost word for word, the indictment already laid against Baasha and Elah in v.13, so that Omri is folded into the same condemnation — walking in all the way of Jeroboam, and in his sin whereby he made Israel to sin, to provoke the Lord God of Israel with their vanities.

The repetition is itself the point. From Jeroboam onward the calf-worship at Dan and Bethel had become the fixed and defining apostasy of every reign, so that the historian need only sound the refrain and the reader hears the whole weight of accumulated provocation behind it. Omri's political success — the founding of Samaria, the alliance with Tyre, the settled dynasty — changes nothing in the divine ledger; measured by the one standard that matters, he is another Jeroboam, and the vanities he served are the same nothings for which his predecessors fell.

The witnesses draw the lesson the chapter has been pressing. Idolatry is not a private failing that a strong ruler may offset with public achievements; it is the provocation that brings a kingdom under God's controversy. The word vanities, recurring here as in v.13, keeps the double edge Ellicott had drawn out — idols are at once an outrage against the living God and an emptiness in themselves, faith squandered on unrealities. Omri, for all his might, spent his reign provoking the Lord with nothings, and his son Ahab, whose accession v.29 announces, will carry that provocation to its dreadful height. The formula, worn smooth by repetition, is the historian's way of saying that nothing had changed but the name of the king. And the witnesses hear in that sameness a warning as much as a verdict: a kingdom may change its capital, its dynasty, and its fortunes, and remain, in the one account that matters, exactly where it was, so long as it will not turn from the vanities that provoke its God.
27:
Omri's reign closes with the annalist's refrain, and the Reformed Gill notes that the rest of his acts, and his might which he showed, were written in the book of the chronicles of the kings of Israel, where those of the preceding kings had been recorded. The formula is by now familiar, but the phrase his might repays the attention the witnesses give it across the chapter.

Omri was, by every indication, a man of real force. He had crushed Zimri, outlasted Tibni in four years of civil war, purchased peace with Syria, allied with Tyre, and founded Samaria on a site chosen by a soldier's eye — achievements that won him a name in the wider records of the ancient world, where, as the commentators note elsewhere, Samaria itself was long known as Beth-Khumri, the house of Omri. The chronicle grants him his might. Yet the very juxtaposition the historian sets up — a notable record of strength standing immediately after the verdict that he did worse than all before him — carries the chapter's steady judgment. Henry's word over the Omri narrative applies exactly here: many a bad man has been a stout man, and his name is found in history and his deeds among the mighty; but such men have no name in the book of life.

The witnesses read the formula, then, as a deliberate anticlimax. Omri's might filled the annals of Israel and even of Assyria, but it could not purchase what his reign most needed — a return to the God he provoked with vanities. His establishment of idolatry by statute (v.25; Micah 6:16) is what heaven remembered of him, while earth remembered his cities and his conquests. The gap between the two memorials is the verse's quiet sermon: strength, success, and a lasting dynasty weigh nothing against the one thing needful, and a king may be mighty in the chronicles of men and still be reckoned, in the account that endures, among those who did evil and passed away. The historian’s bare juxtaposition of Omri’s might and Omri’s guilt needs no added censure; it leaves the reader to measure the whole weight of a life that was strong in everything but the fear of God.
28:
Omri's death is recorded in the settled phrase of the narrative, and the Reformed Gill gives the plain sense: So Omri slept with his fathers — he died a natural death — and, as the verse adds, was buried in Samaria, the city he himself had founded, and Ahab his son reigned in his stead. Like Baasha before him (v.6), the founder of a dynasty goes down to the grave in peace, in his own royal city, undisturbed by any visible stroke of the judgment his idolatry deserved.

The witnesses feel again the tension that Henry had pressed at Baasha's burial. Omri, who did worse than all that were before him and bound a whole people to idolatry by statute, dies quietly and is honourably interred in the capital that would carry his name for centuries. The peace of his end is no measure of the state of his soul; it is the reprieve of a patient God, who often lets the wicked prosper to the grave and reserves the reckoning for a day beyond it. That Omri is buried in Samaria — the monument of his political genius and the future seat of Baal-worship — only sharpens the irony: his greatest achievement becomes his tomb.

The verse also serves as a threshold, naming for the first time in the narrative the son who will eclipse every predecessor in wickedness. Ahab his son reigned in his stead: the way for that reign, as Keil and Delitzsch observe over the following lines, had already been prepared by Omri himself, so that Ahab's turning for the worse is the ripening of a seed his father planted. The witnesses read Omri's peaceful death and honourable burial not as the closing of an account but as the passing of a settled and prosperous throne into hands that would use its very stability to establish a fouler idolatry — the marriage with Jezebel and the temple of Baal that v.31 and v.32 will describe. The witnesses read Omri’s quiet death, then, less as an ending than as a handing-on: the strong and settled kingdom he leaves behind is the very instrument by which his son will carry Israel’s apostasy to its darkest depth, so that the founder’s success becomes, in the providence of God, the stage for a still greater sin.
29:
The narrative reaches the reign it has been building toward, and the witnesses open the account of Ahab with a sense of foreboding. The Reformed Gill fixes the date — Ahab began to reign in the thirty-eighth year of Asa, the same year his father died — and notes the length that made his mischief possible: twenty-two years. The Puritan Matthew Henry, whose great reflection governs the rest of the chapter, observes that of Ahab more particulars are recorded than of any king of Israel, and that this general notice serves as a warning of what the particulars will be: he was the worst of them all, and reigned long enough to do a great deal of harm.

Henry draws out the terrible progression the verse announces. Ahab exceeded all his predecessors in wickedness, doing evil above all that were before him, and, as if with a particular enmity against both God and Israel, did more to provoke the Lord than all the kings before him (v.33). It was a bad state of the people, Henry reflects, when every successive king was worse than the last; what would they come to at length? Ahab had seen the ruin of other wicked houses, yet instead of taking warning his heart was hardened. He thought it a light thing to walk in the sins of Jeroboam (v.31): it was nothing to him to break the second commandment by image-worship, so he would set aside the first also by bringing in other gods.

From this Henry presses the searching principle the witnesses share — that making light of lesser sins makes way for greater, and that those who labour to extenuate other men's sins do but aggravate their own. Ahab's little finger, in the prophet's manner of speaking, would fall heavier on God's ordinances than Jeroboam's loins. The verse thus stands as the somber overture to the darkest reign in the northern kingdom: a king who inherited a settled throne and a prepared apostasy and gave himself, with deliberate enmity, to carry both to their ruinous height, so that the reading of the chapter, Henry concludes, should make us mark the dreadful end of all the workers of iniquity.
30:
The historian's verdict on Ahab is stated flatly, and the witnesses weigh the superlative it shares with the verdict on Omri. The Reformed Gill locates Ahab's distinctive guilt precisely: he did evil above all that were before him by adding other idols to the calves, and idols more abominable than they, since the earlier kings had at least pretended to worship the true God under the image of an ox, whereas Ahab worshipped other gods besides him, as the following verses show.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch press the Hebrew idiom to sharpen the same point. The former kings had only perpetuated Jeroboam's calf-worship — the worship of Jehovah under the image of an ox — but Ahab was not satisfied with this. The construction, they explain, carries the sense was it too little?: because it was too light a thing for him to walk in the sins of Jeroboam, he took Jezebel the daughter of Ethbaal king of the Sidonians to wife, and served Baal, and worshipped him. The pictorial he went and served, they note, marks the decisive new turn of affairs; and they identify Ethbaal, from Menander in Josephus, as the priest of Astarte who usurped the throne of Tyre and Sidon and reigned thirty-two years.

The witnesses agree on the nature of the descent the verse announces. Under Jeroboam and his successors the sin of Israel had been a corrupt worship of the true God; under Ahab it becomes the worship of a false one. The calves had broken the second commandment; Baal breaks the first. This is the line Ahab crosses, and it is why the historian, having called Omri worse than all before him, must now say the same and more of the son — not a difference of degree merely but of kind. The bare statement of this verse is the hinge; v.31 will name the marriage and the god by which Ahab earned it, and the whole tragic conflict between Baal and Jehovah that fills the following chapters is here set in motion. The witnesses agree that the historian’s superlative is therefore no mere rhetorical heightening: it registers a real threshold crossed, the moment when Israel’s sin ceased to be a debased worship of its own God and became the open service of another.
31:
The fatal step of Ahab's reign is named, and the witnesses concentrate on the marriage and the worship it brought. The Reformed Gill takes the opening clause as the historian's own astonishment — as if it had been a light thing to walk in Jeroboam's sin of the golden calves, Ahab went yet further. The Wesleyan Clarke fixes on Jezebel as the head and chief of his offending: he took to wife not merely a heathen but the idolatrous daughter of an idolatrous king, whose hostility to the true religion was open, protected, rewarded, and cruel — she put to death the prophets and priests of God with zealous perseverance, so that his marrying her was esteemed the highest pitch of vice.

The grammatical-historical commentators supply the history and the theology. Keil and Delitzsch identify the Asherah of v.33 as an idol of Astarte and Ethbaal as the usurping priest-king of Tyre. Ellicott expands the picture: Ethbaal, the Eithobalus of Menander, had assassinated the king of Tyre and founded a new dynasty, and was a priest of Astarte, styled not king of Tyre but king of the Sidonians; his priestly origin may account for the fanatic devotion to Baal in Jezebel and Athaliah, in marked contrast with the friendlier religious attitude of Hiram (1 Kings 5:7; 2 Chron. 2:12). The marriage, Ellicott judges, was the fatal turning-point in the life of a man physically brave and possibly able, but morally weak and impressible for good and evil alike — the almost complete supremacy of Jezebel's strong, relentless nature over his, a contrast he likens to Macbeth and Lady Macbeth.

On the god himself Ellicott is careful. Baal means only Lord, a mere title marked by its usual article, and applied in various mythologies; hence the plural Baalim of Canaanite worship (Judges 2:11; 1 Sam. 7:4), and even, in Hosea 2:16, the word is used of Jehovah himself. But the Phoenician Baal, worshipped as the productive principle of nature alongside Astarte, was now introduced on a great scale with profuse magnificence and enforced by Jezebel with a high hand and persecution of the Lord's prophets, so that, Ellicott concludes, the conflict between it and the spiritual worship of Jehovah became a conflict of life and death. The witnesses do not divide; they see in this one marriage the source of Israel's deadliest apostasy.
32:
Ahab gives his new worship a settled home, and the Reformed Gill draws out the significance of the single verse. Ahab reared up an altar for Baal in the house of Baal, which he had built in Samaria: he built a temple in his own royal city, Gill observes, both that he might not have to travel as far as Tyre or Sidon to worship, and for his wife's convenience, and there erected an altar to offer sacrifices to Baal — so open and daring, Gill remarks, were his idolatrous practices.

The verse marks a decisive advance in the apostasy the chapter has traced. The calf-worship of Jeroboam had at least kept the name and the pretence of Jehovah; now a rival temple with its own altar rises in the capital, a public establishment of a foreign god at the very seat of the kingdom. Henry, reflecting over the passage, catches the calculated affront: Ahab built Baal's temple in Samaria, the royal city, because the temple of the true God stood in Jerusalem, the royal city of the other kingdom; he would have Baal's house near him, the better to frequent it, protect it, and put honour upon it. The altar, by which the worshippers acknowledged their dependence on the idol and sought his favour, made the break with the God of Israel complete and official.

The witnesses read the reared altar as the visible monument of the marriage of v.31. What Jezebel brought as a foreign bride's religion Ahab has now enthroned as a state cult, housed and endowed at public cost in the city Omri built. Henry marvels at the stupidity of idolaters who are at great expense to make a friend of one whom they might have chosen never to reckon a god at all. The single altar in Samaria is thus the hinge on which the following history turns: from it will spring the prophets of Baal, the persecution of the Lord's servants, and the great contest on Carmel, for here, in a sentence, the worship of Baal has ceased to be Jezebel's private devotion and become the official religion of the northern throne.
33:
Ahab crowns his idolatry, and the witnesses gather on the meaning of the grove and the height of provocation the verse records. The Reformed Gill and the Wesleyan Clarke agree that grove renders the Hebrew Asherah, and is better taken as the goddess Astarte, the deity of the Sidonians, an image of which Ahab made — Clarke identifying her with Astarte or Venus, and, from the Syriac and Arabic, with an idol set among tall trees, perhaps a figure of Priapus, the obscene keeper of groves and gardens. Gill adds the darker note that in such a grove, whether of natural or planted trees, all manner of filthiness was secretly committed, the shade contrived to conceal and countenance the impurities of Baal's worship.

The single grammatical-historical comment here is the terse verdict of Jamieson, Fausset and Brown, who read Ahab's act as a sad proof of the maniacal frenzy into which the unhappy monarch was transported — idolatry pursued now not as policy but as a kind of madness. The phrase catches what the whole verse asserts: that Ahab did more to provoke the Lord God of Israel to anger than all the kings of Israel that were before him, the superlative of v.30 now driven to its furthest point.

Henry, over the passage, presses the moral. The grove was made, not merely planted, that something might answer the intention of concealment — and he turns the Latin proverb, a grove is so called from its want of light, into the Gospel word that he that doeth evil hateth the light. The witnesses do not divide; together they see in the Asherah beside the altar the last step of a deliberate descent: image-worship, then a foreign god, then a temple and altar, and now the obscene rites of Astarte established in Israel. It was done, Henry observes, as if purposely to provoke the Lord and so bring judgment on the land, and the following chapters — the drought, Elijah, and Carmel — are the answer of the God so provoked. The witnesses read the Asherah beside Baal’s altar as the sign that Ahab’s idolatry had passed beyond calculation into compulsion, the very frenzy of which Jamieson, Fausset and Brown spoke, which no motive of policy could any longer explain.
34:
The chapter ends with a strange and terrible sign, and the witnesses read Hiel's building of Jericho as the measure of how far impiety had spread and how surely God's word stands. The Reformed Gill notes that Hiel the Bethelite, either ignorant of Joshua's ancient curse or in defiance of it — and knowing it might please Ahab and Jezebel — rebuilt the city under royal authority, which is why it is set among Ahab's wicked deeds. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch treat the fulfilment as the point: he laid the foundation in Abiram his firstborn and set up the gates in Segub his youngest, according to the word of the Lord which he spake by Joshua the son of Nun (Joshua 6:26), so that the rebuilding shows how far ungodliness had advanced, and the death of the builder's sons how the Lord will not let the word of his servants be transgressed with impunity.

Ellicott sets the deed in its political frame. The rebuilding marks both the growth of prosperity and power and the neglect of the old curse; Jericho, though not wholly deserted before (Judges 3:13; 2 Sam. 10:5), was now made an important place, of great natural advantages and military consequence, commanding the pass from the Jordan valley to the highlands. Having been assigned to Benjamin (Joshua 18:21), it should properly have belonged to Judah, and its rebuilding by a Bethelite under Ahab's patronage betrays the half-dependent condition of the southern kingdom at that time.

The one open debate is over the manner of the curse's fulfilment, and the Wesleyan Clarke lays out three views: that Hiel's children died one by one, the eldest at the founding and the youngest at the hanging of the gates, so that his whole family perished; that the words mean only a long and hindered labour, the builder spending his whole procreative life on the work; or that Hiel, in a heathen rite, sacrificed his firstborn at the foundation and his youngest at the completion — the view the Chaldee favours. Clarke inclines to the reading of delay, doubting that the innocent children suffered for their father's presumption; Keil and the others take the sons' deaths as the direct stroke of the curse. Henry, drawing the chapter's close, likens Hiel to Achan meddling with the accursed thing, and finds the lesson plain: those whom God curses are cursed indeed, and none ever hardened his heart against God and prospered. So the history of Israel's basest reign ends on a note of warning, that God keeps back his people from presumptuous sins.
